Lesson 0 - Getting Started

What computer do you use?

Chromebook

Chromebooks are inexpensive laptops that run Chrome OS, but they are not great for web development. It is possible to install Linux on some Chromebooks, but Google makes it difficult to do this without opening the computer up and making changes to the hardware. This will, at the very least, void your warranty and I honestly do not recommend doing this. I destroyed one Chromebook when I tried. If you want a cheap laptop for web development, consider a Windows or Linux machine instead.

Android

Android phones and tablets are not ideal for web development. While there are some code editors available for Android, the experience is limited compared to using a laptop or desktop computer. If possible, use a Windows, or Linux machine for the best experience.

iOS (iPhone/iPad)

Apple iPhones and iPads are not ideal for web development. While there are some code editors available for iOS, the experience is limited compared to using a laptop or desktop computer. If possible, use a macOS machine such as an iMac or MacBook for the best experience.

Introduction

In this lesson, you will ensure that you have all the necessary tools installed on your computer to start learning web development. By the end of this lesson, you will be ready to create your first web page. If you are at a Code Dojo and are using a club computer, these tools will already be installed for you so you can skip to the next lesson.

Warning

This lesson involves installing software on your computer. If you are using a shared or school computer, please check with a parent, guardian, or teacher before proceeding with the installations. Also, you will need administrative privileges to install the software. This means you will need an administrator password at various stages. If you are not using your own computer, you may need to get the owner to do those steps for you. Finally be aware that the instructions are long and detailed to ensure you get everything set up correctly. Take your time and follow each step carefully. Ask for help from a mentor, if one is available. The rest of the lessons are much shorter and easier once you have the tools installed!

Installing Required Software

These are the tools you need to install. Some may already be installed on your computer, in which case you can skip those sections. You may use other tools if you prefer, but these are the ones the lessons will assume you have.

  • Mozilla Firefox (or another modern web browser such as Google Chrome or Apple Safari).
  • Microsoft Visual Studio Code (or another code editor).
  • Node.js (required for running the development server).

Other tools will be introduced in later lessons, but these are the basics you need to get started. Follow the instructions for your operating system to install these tools.

Windows Installation Instructions

The latest version of Windows is Windows 11, but you should be able to use Windows 10 as well. The lessons have not been tested on earlier versions of Windows, but they may work ok. It's possible that the browser on older versions of windows won't run all the features used in the lessons. In particular, beware of using Internet Explorer - it is not supported any more and Microsoft Edge is recommended instead. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your Windows computer.

Mozilla Firefox

Check whether Firefox is already installed

  1. Click the Start button (Windows logo in the bottom-left corner)
  2. Type "Firefox"
  3. If you see it appear in the search results with an orange/red fox icon, it's already installed!
  4. If you don't see it, continue to the installation steps below

If you need to install Firefox, follow these steps

  1. Download Firefox from the Official Website
    1. Open your current web browser (like Edge or Chrome)
    2. Go to: https://www.mozilla.org/firefox/
    3. Click the big "Download Firefox" button
    4. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like Firefox Installer.exe)
  2. Run the Installer
    1. Find the downloaded file in your Downloads folder
    2. Double-click the file to start the installation
    3. If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Follow the Setup Wizard
    1. Click Install on the welcome screen (the installer uses smart defaults)
    2. Wait for the installation to finish (this takes about 30 seconds to 1 minute)
    3. Firefox will open automatically when installation is complete
    4. Congratulations! You've installed Firefox!
  4. First time opening Firefox
    1. Firefox will show a welcome page
    2. You might be asked:
      • "Make Firefox your default browser?" - This means Firefox will open when you click links. You can choose Yes or Skip (you can change this later)
      • "Set Up Sync?" - This lets you save bookmarks to your account. You can choose Not now if you want (you can do this later)
    3. That's it! Firefox is ready to use!

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed

  1. Click the Start button
  2. Type "cmd" or "Command Prompt"
  3. Click to open Command Prompt (a black window will appear)
  4. Type this command and press Enter: 
    
  code --version
  5. If you see version numbers (like 1.95.0), VS Code is already installed! Close this window and skip the installation.
  6. If you see an error like "'code' is not recognised", VS Code is not installed yet - continue to the next section.

If you need to install Visual Studio Code, follow these steps

  1. Download VS Code from the Official Website
    1. Open your web browser (like Firefox)
    2. Go to: https://code.visualstudio.com
    3. Click the big blue "Download for Windows" button
    4. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like VSCodeUserSetup-x64-1.xx.x.exe)
  2. Run the Installer
    1. Find the downloaded file in your Downloads folder
    2. Double-click the file to start the installation
    3. If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Follow the Setup Wizard
    1. Click Next on the welcome screen
    2. Click I accept the agreement, then click Next
    3. Keep clicking Next (the default settings are fine)
    4. On the "Select Additional Tasks" page, make sure these boxes are checked:
      • Create a desktop icon
      • Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer file context menu
      • Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer directory context menu
      • Add to PATH
    5. Click Next, then click Install
    6. Wait for the installation to finish (this takes about 1-2 minutes)
    7. Click Finish (you can leave "Launch Visual Studio Code" checked)
  4. Select First time opening Visual Studio Code
    1. VS Code will open automatically (or double-click the desktop icon)
    2. You'll see a welcome screen - you can close this for now
    3. Congratulations! You've installed VS Code!

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed

  1. Click the Start button
  2. Type "cmd" or "Command Prompt"
  3. Click to open Command Prompt (a black window will appear)
  4. Type this command and press Enter: 
    
node --version
  5. If you see a version number (like v22.0.0 or v20.11.0), Node.js is already installed!
  6. If you see an error like "'node' is not recognised", Node.js is not installed yet - continue to the next section.

If you need to install Node.js, follow these steps

  1. Download Node.js from the Official Website
    1. Open your web browser (like Firefox)
    2. Go to: https://nodejs.org
    3. You'll see two big green buttons. Click the one that says "LTS" (Long Term Support)
      • LTS means it's the most stable and recommended version
      • The button will say something like "20.11.0 LTS (Recommended For Most Users)"
    4. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like node-v20.11.0-x64.msi)
  2. Run the Installer
    1. Find the downloaded file in your Downloads folder
    2. Double-click the file to start the installation
    3. If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Follow the setup wizard
    1. Click Next on the welcome screen
    2. Click I accept the license agreement, then click Next
    3. Choose where to install - keep the default location and click Next
    4. Custom Setup - keep all the default options selected (everything should have a checkmark)
      • Node.js runtime
      • npm package manager
      • Online documentation shortcuts
      • Add to PATH
    5. Click Next
    6. Tools for Native Modules - You'll see a checkbox about installing additional tools
      • You can uncheck this box for now (you won't need it yet)
    7. Click Next, then click Install
    8. Wait for the installation to finish (this takes about 1-2 minutes)
    9. Click Finish
  4. Verify Installation
    1. Open Command Prompt again (or close and reopen it if it was already open)
    2. Type these commands one at a time and press Enter after each: 
      
node --version
      You should see something like v20.11.0
    3. Type the following command and press Enter: 
      
npm --version
      You should see something like 10.2.4
    4. If you see version numbers for both, congratulations! Node.js is installed correctly!

Create a Projects Folder

Projects folder is a special place on your computer where you keep all your coding projects organised. It's like having a toy box where you keep all your toys in one place - it makes everything easier to find!

Why Do You Need It?

When you start coding, you'll create lots of different projects. Having them all in one folder helps you:

  • Find your projects quickly
  • Keep your computer organised
  • Back up your work easily
  • Know where to save new projects
  1. Open File Explorer
    1. Click the folder icon on your taskbar (bottom of screen)
    2. Or press the Windows key + E on your keyboard
  2. Navigate to Your User Folder
    1. Look on the left side of File Explorer
    2. Click on "This PC" or "Quick access"
    3. Find and click on your user folder (it has your name and a person icon)
      • It might be called something like "Documents" or your Windows username
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    1. Right-click in an empty space in the window
    2. Hover over "New" in the menu that appears
    3. Click "Folder"
    4. A new folder appears with the name highlighted
    5. Type: Projects
    6. Press Enter

Your Projects folder is now at: C:\Users\YourUsername\Projects

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

An HTTP server is a program that serves web pages to your browser. When you visit a website, you're connecting to an HTTP server! So here you'll create your own server that runs on your computer that you can use to view your web pages as you develop them.

Before You Start

Make sure you have:

  • Node.js installed (check by typing node --version in Command Prompt)
  • A Projects folder created (see "How to Create a Projects Folder" guide)

Download package.json File

  1. Open Firefox (or your web browser)
  2. Right click on this link: package.json and select Save Link As...
  3. Save the file to your Downloads folder as package.json
  4. The file will download to your Downloads folder
  5. Open File Explorer (click the folder icon on your taskbar)
  6. Go to your Downloads folder
  7. Find package.json and right-click on it
  8. Choose Cut (or press Ctrl + X)
  9. Navigate to your Projects folder
  10. Right-click in an empty space and choose Paste (or press Ctrl + V)

Start the server

  1. In Command Prompt, type: 
    
npm run start
  2. You should see: Server running at http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and visit: http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see: "Hello! Your server is working!"

Stop the server

  1. Go back to the Command Prompt
  2. Press Ctrl + C
  3. The server will stop

Using the Development Server

  • Whenever you need to start the server, run npm start from your Projects folder
  • When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  • When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  • To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  • To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder
  • In future lessons, when you have learned about Visual Studio Code, you can use a Terminal inside VS Code to run these commands

macOS Installation Instructions

The latest version of Apple's macOS is macOS Ventura, but you should be able to use recent versions as well. The lessons have not been tested on earlier versions of macOS, but they may work ok. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your macOS computer.

Mozilla Firefox

Check whether Firefox is already installed:

  1. Open Finder (click the smiling face icon in your Dock)
  2. Click Applications in the left sidebar
  3. Scroll through the list looking for Firefox (orange/red fox icon)
  4. If you find it, Firefox is already installed!
  5. If you don't see it, continue to the installation steps below

If you need to install Firefox, follow these steps:

  1. Download Firefox from the Official Website
    1. Open your current web browser (like Safari)
    2. Go to: https://www.mozilla.org/firefox/
    3. Click the big "Download Firefox" button
    4. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like Firefox xx.x.dmg)
    5. The file will be saved to your Downloads folder
  2. Install Firefox
    1. Find the Firefox.dmg file in your Downloads folder (or click it in your Downloads list)
    2. Double-click the .dmg file to open it
    3. A window will appear showing the Firefox icon and an Applications folder
    4. Drag the Firefox icon into the Applications folder
    5. Wait for Firefox to copy (this takes about 10-30 seconds)
    6. Once copying is complete, eject the Firefox disk image:
      • Click the eject button next to "Firefox" in Finder's sidebar
      • Or drag the Firefox disk image to the Trash
  3. Open Firefox for the first time
    1. Open Finder
    2. Click Applications
    3. Find and double-click Firefox
    4. You might see a message: "Firefox is an app downloaded from the internet. Are you sure you want to open it?"
      • Click Open (this is safe - Firefox is from Mozilla's official website)
    5. Firefox will open!
  4. First time setup
    1. Firefox will show a welcome page
    2. You might be asked:
      • "Make Firefox your default browser?" - This means Firefox will open when you click links. You can choose Set as Default or Not now (you can change this later)
      • "Set Up Firefox Sync?" - This lets you save bookmarks to your account. You can choose Not now if you want (you can do this later)
    3. That's it! Firefox is ready to use!
  5. Add to Dock (Optional)
    This will make it easy to open Firefox in the future by clicking its icon in the Dock at the bottom of your screen.
    1. With Firefox open, look at its icon in the Dock
    2. Right-click (or Control-click) the Firefox icon in the Dock
    3. Choose Options → Keep in Dock
    4. Now Firefox will always stay in your Dock!

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed:

  1. Press Command (⌘) + Space to open Spotlight
  2. Type "Visual Studio Code" or "VS Code"
  3. If you see it appear with a blue icon, it's already installed!
  4. If you don't see it, continue to the installation steps below

If you need to install Visual Studio Code, follow these steps:

  1. Download VS Code from the Official Website
    1. Open your web browser (like Safari or Firefox)
    2. Go to: https://code.visualstudio.com
    3. Click the big blue "Download Mac Universal" button
    4. This version works on both Intel and Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) Macs
    5. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like VSCode-darwin-universal.zip)
    6. The file will be saved to your Downloads folder
  2. Install VS Code
    1. Find the VSCode-darwin-universal.zip file in your Downloads folder
    2. Double-click the .zip file to extract it
    3. You'll see a Visual Studio Code.app file appear
    4. Drag the Visual Studio Code app to your Applications folder
    5. You can open a new Finder window and click Applications in the sidebar
    6. Or drag it to the Applications shortcut in the Downloads folder window
  3. Open VS Code for the first time
    1. Open Finder
    2. Click Applications
    3. Find and double-click Visual Studio Code
    4. You might see a message: "Visual Studio Code is an app downloaded from the internet. Are you sure you want to open it?"
      • Click Open (this is safe - VS Code is from Microsoft's official website)
    5. VS Code will open!
  4. Add to Dock (Optional)
    This will make it easy to open VS Code in the future by clicking its icon in the Dock at the bottom of your screen.
    1. With VS Code open, look at its icon in the Dock
    2. Right-click (or Control-click) the VS Code icon in the Dock
    3. Choose Options → Keep in Dock
    4. Now VS Code will always stay in your Dock!
  5. Install the 'code' Command (Optional but Recommended)
    This allows you to open VS Code directly from the Terminal by typing code.
    1. In VS Code, press Command (⌘) + Shift + P to open the Command Palette
    2. Type "shell command"
    3. Click "Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH"
    4. Enter your password if asked
    5. You'll see a message saying it was installed successfully

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed:

  1. Press Command (⌘) + Space to open Spotlight
  2. Type Terminal
  3. Press Return to open Terminal
  4. Type this command and press Return: 
    node --version
  5. If you see a version number (like v22.0.0 or v20.11.0), Node.js is already installed!
  6. If you see an error like "command not found", Node.js is not installed yet - continue to the next section.

If you need to install Node.js, follow these steps:

  1. Download Node.js
    1. Open your web browser (like Safari or Firefox)
    2. Go to: https://nodejs.org
    3. You'll see two big green buttons. Click the one that says "LTS" (Long Term Support)
      • LTS means it's the most stable and recommended version
      • The button will say something like "20.11.0 LTS (Recommended For Most Users)"
    4. Wait for the file to download (it's called something like node-v20.11.0.pkg)
    5. The file will be saved to your Downloads folder
  2. Install Node.js
    1. Find the downloaded .pkg file in your Downloads folder
    2. Double-click the file to start the installation
    3. If macOS asks for your password, enter it (this is your Mac login password)
  3. Follow the Installation Wizard
    1. Click Continue on the welcome screen
    2. Click Continue again on the license screen
    3. Click Agree to accept the license
    4. Choose where to install - keep the default location and click Continue
    5. Click Install (you may need to enter your password again)
    6. Wait for the installation to finish (this takes about 1-2 minutes)
    7. Click Close
    8. You can move the installer to Trash if asked
  4. Verify Installation
    1. Open Terminal (or close and reopen it if it was already open)
      • Press Command (⌘) + Space, type "Terminal", press Return
    2. Type these commands one at a time and press Return after each: 
      
node --version
      You should see something like v20.11.0 
      
npm --version
      You should see something like 10.2.4
    3. If you see version numbers for both, congratulations! Node.js is installed correctly!

Create a Projects Folder

Projects folder is a special place on your computer where you keep all your coding projects organised. It's like having a toy box where you keep all your toys in one place - it makes everything easier to find!

Why Do You Need It?

When you start coding, you'll create lots of different projects. Having them all in one folder helps you:

  • Find your projects quickly
  • Keep your computer organised
  • Back up your work easily
  • Know where to save new projects
  1. Open Finder
    1. Click the Finder icon in your Dock (the smiling blue and white face)
    2. Or press Command (⌘) + Space to open Spotlight, type Finder, and press Enter
  2. Go to Your Home Folder
    1. In Finder, click Go in the menu bar at the top
    2. Click Home (or press Shift + Command + H)
    3. You're now in your home folder (it has your username and a house icon)
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    1. Right-click (or Control-click) in an empty space in the window
    2. Choose New Folder from the menu
    3. A new folder appears with the name highlighted
    4. Type: Projects
    5. Press Return (Enter)

Your Projects folder is in your home folder, which is usually here: /Users/YourName/Projects (Replace "YourName" with your actual macOS username)

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

An HTTP server is a program that serves web pages to your browser. When you visit a website, you're connecting to an HTTP server! Today, you'll create your own server that runs on your computer.

Before You Start

Make sure you have:

  • Node.js installed (check by typing node --version in Terminal)
  • A Projects folder created (see "How to Create a Projects Folder" guide)

Download package.json File

  1. Open Firefox or Safari
  2. Right click on this link: package.json and select Save Link As...
  3. Save the file to your Downloads folder as package.json
  4. The file will download to your Downloads folder
  5. Open Finder (click the Finder icon on your dock)
  6. Go to your Downloads folder
  7. Find package.json and right-click on it
  8. Choose Cut (or press Command + X)
  9. Navigate to your Projects folder
  10. Right-click in an empty space and choose Paste (or press Command + V)

Open a terminal in your Projects folder

  1. Open Terminal (press Command (⌘) + Space, type "Terminal", press Return)
  2. Type these commands: 
    
cd ~/Projects
  3. You should now be in your Projects folder (you can check by typing pwd and pressing Enter - it should show something like /Users/YourName/Projects)
  4. cd cd means "change directory" (move to that folder)
  5. ~ is a shortcut for your home folder

Start the server

  1. In Terminal, type: 
    
npm run start
  2. You should see: 
    
Server running at http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and visit: http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see: "Hello! Your server is working!" 🎉
  5. Keep the terminal open while you're working - the server needs to run continuously
  6. When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  7. When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  8. To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  9. To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder

Linux Installation Instructions

There are many different versions of Linux available, so these instructions will cover the most common distributions: Debian, Fedora and ArchLinux. If you are using a different distribution, the steps may vary slightly. The lessons have been tested on Linux Mint 22.2 (which is based on Ubuntu 22.04) but should work fine with other flavours and versions. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your Linux computer.

To determine which Linux distribution you are using, open a terminal and type:


cat /etc/os-release | grep -E 'ID=|ID_LIKE='
  • If you see debian or ubuntu, you are using a Debian-based distribution
  • If you see fedora or rhel, you are using a Fedora-based distribution
  • If you see arch, you are using an Arch-based distribution

Using Super User (sudo)

On Linux, some commands require special permissions to run. This is for security reasons - it prevents accidental changes to important system files. When you see a command that starts with sudo, it means "run this command as a super user (administrator)".

Not all users have super user privileges. You can check if you have sudo access by pressing Ctrl + Alt + T to open Terminal and typing: 


sudo echo "sudo works"

If you are prompted for a password, enter your user password. If you see the message sudo works, you have sudo access! If you see an error like user is not in the sudoers file, you don't have sudo access and will need to log in as a user who does.

When you run a command with sudo, you'll be asked to enter your password. This is the same password you use to log in to your Linux account. When you type it, you won't see any characters appear - this is normal! Just type your password and press Enter.

Mozilla Firefox

Good news! Firefox usually comes pre-installed on Linux, so you might already have it!

Check whether Firefox is already installed:

  1. Press Ctrl + Alt + T to open Terminal
  2. Type this command and press Enter: 
    
firefox --version
  3. If you see a version number (like "Mozilla Firefox 121.0"), Firefox is already installed!
  4. If you see "command not found", continue to the installation steps below

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Debian or Ubuntu Linux, follow these steps:

  1. Press Ctrl + Alt + T to open Terminal
  2. Update your package list and install Firefox: 
    
sudo apt update
sudo apt install firefox -y
firefox --version
    • Enter your password when prompted (you won't see it being typed - this is normal!)
    • You should see the Firefox version number

Done! Firefox is installed!

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Fedora or Red Hat-based Linux, follow these steps:

  1. Click Activities in the top-left corner
  2. Type "Software" and press Enter
  3. In GNOME Software, search for "Firefox"
  4. Click on Firefox Web Browser
  5. Click the Install button
  6. Enter your password when prompted
  7. Wait for the installation to complete (this takes 1-3 minutes)
  8. Click Launch to open Firefox

Arch Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Arch Linux, follow these steps:

  1. Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  2. Update your system first and install Firefox: 
    
sudo pacman -Syu
sudo pacman -S firefox
firefox --version
    • Enter your password when prompted (you won't see it being typed - this is normal!)
    • Press Enter to confirm installation

Done! Firefox is installed!

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed:

  1. Press Ctrl + Alt + T to open Terminal
  2. Type this command and press Enter: 
    
code --version
  3. If you see version numbers (like 1.95.0), VS Code is already installed!
  4. If you see an error like "command not found", VS Code is not installed yet - continue to the next section.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Visual Studio Code on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Press Ctrl + Alt + T to open Terminal
  2. Update package list and install dependencies: 
    
sudo apt update
sudo apt install wget gpg -y
wget -qO- https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | gpg --dearmor > packages.microsoft.gpg
sudo install -D -o root -g root -m 644 packages.microsoft.gpg /etc/apt/keyrings/packages.microsoft.gpg
echo "deb [arch=amd64,arm64,armhf signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/packages.microsoft.gpg] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/code stable main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/vscode.list > /dev/null
sudo apt update
sudo apt install code -y

Done! VS Code is installed!

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Visual Studio Code on Fedora, follow these steps:

  1. Open Terminal
  2. Import Microsoft's GPG key: 
    
sudo rpm --import https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc
sudo sh -c 'echo -e "[code]
name=Visual Studio Code
baseurl=https://packages.microsoft.com/yumrepos/vscode
enabled=1
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc" > /etc/yum.repos.d/vscode.repo'
  3. Update package cache and install VS Code:
    • For RHEL 8+ and Fedora (DNF): 
      
sudo dnf check-update
sudo dnf install code -y
    • For RHEL 7 (YUM): 
      
sudo yum check-update
sudo yum install code -y

Done! VS Code is installed!

Arch Linux

If you need to install Visual Studio Code on Arch Linux, follow these steps:

  1. Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  2. Update your system first: 
    
sudo pacman -Syu
yay -S visual-studio-code-bin
code --version

Done! VS Code is installed!

Launch VS Code

  • Type code in Terminal and press Enter, or
  • Find "Visual Studio Code" in your application menu
  • Click to open VS Code

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type node --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Node.js on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Install Node.js via NodeSource Repository (Recommended)
    • Open Terminal
    • Run these commands: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install curl
curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash -
sudo apt install nodejs
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Alternative: Install from Ubuntu Repositories
    • Note: This may install an older version
    • Open Terminal
    • Run: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nodejs npm
  3. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Node.js on Fedora:

  1. Install Node.js
    • Open Terminal
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf install nodejs npm
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum install nodejs npm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Arch Linux

If you're using Arch Linux:

  1. Install Node.js
    • Open Terminal
    • Run: 
      
sudo pacman -S nodejs npm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Using Node Version Manager (nvm) - Advanced Option

For more control over Node.js versions:

  1. Install nvm
    • Run this command: 
      
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash
    • Close and reopen your terminal
  2. Install Node.js with nvm
    • Install the LTS version: 
      
nvm install --lts
    • Set it as default: 
      
nvm use --lts
  3. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test directory: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
      • Create a test file: 
        
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
      • Start the server: 
        
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Create a Projects Folder

It's helpful to keep all your coding projects organised in one place. Let's create a dedicated Projects folder:

  1. Open Terminal
    • Press Ctrl + Alt + T
  2. Navigate to Your Home Directory
    • The terminal usually starts in your home directory
    • To confirm, type pwd and press Enter
    • You should see something like /home/yourusername
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    • Type this command and press Enter: 
      
mkdir Projects
    • Verify it was created: 
      
ls
    • You should see Projects in the list

Your Projects folder is now at: ~/Projects (or /home/yourusername/Projects)

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

+

To view your web pages as you develop them, you'll need to run a local development server. This is important because some web features don't work when opening HTML files directly in a browser. If you installed the Live Server extension in Visual Studio Code, you can skip this step as that will do the same thing. Using this method lets you run and test your web pages even if you are not using Visual Studio Code.

Create a package.json File

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Select File → Open Folder
  3. Navigate to and select your Projects folder (~/Projects)
  4. Create a new file called package.json in the Projects folder
  5. Add the following content to the file:
  6. Save the file (Ctrl + S)

Install http-server

  1. Open the integrated terminal in VS Code by pressing Ctrl + ` (backtick key, usually above Tab)
  2. Make sure you're in your Projects folder (the terminal should show the path)
  3. Type npm install and press Enter
  4. Wait for the installation to complete (this installs http-server locally in your Projects folder)

Start the Development Server

  1. In the same terminal, type npm start and press Enter
  2. You should see a message saying the server is running on http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see a directory listing of your Projects folder

Using the Development Server

  • Keep the terminal open while you're working - the server needs to run continuously
  • When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  • When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  • To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  • To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder

Conclusion

Congratulations! You have installed all the necessary tools to start your web development journey. In the next lesson, you will create your first web page using HTML and CSS.
